epigraph.text: For dust you are and to dust you will return.

content.text:
Many natural processes—population growth, spread of disease, compound 
interest—follow exponential patterns. When the rate of change is 
proportional to the current amount, we get exponential growth or decay.

content.title: Exponential Growth/Decay Model
content.content:
The general exponential model is:
\[
 N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}
\]
where:

- $N(t)$ = amount at time $t$

- $N_0$ = initial amount (when $t = 0$)

- $k$ = growth/decay constant

- If $k > 0$: exponential **growth**

- If $k < 0$: exponential **decay**

content.title: Finding $k$ from data
content.problem:
A bacteria culture grows from 500 to 1500 in 3 hours. Find the growth 
constant $k$ and predict the population after 8 hours.

content.text: Two important quantities characterize exponential processes:

content.title: Doubling Time
content.content:
The **doubling time** is the time for a quantity 
to double:
\[
 t_{\text{double}} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k}
\]

content.title: Half-Life
content.content:
The **half-life** is the time for a quantity to 
reduce to half:
\[
 t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{|k|} \approx \frac{0.693}{|k|}
\]

content.text:
Notice that doubling time and half-life use the same formula—they're 
inverse concepts for growth and decay processes.

content.title: Finding half-life
content.problem:
Carbon-14 decays with a rate constant of $k \approx -0.000121$ per year. 
Find its half-life.
content.solution:
\[
 t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{0.000121} \approx \frac{0.693}{0.000121} \approx 5730 \text{ years}
\]

This means half of any carbon-14 sample decays every 5730 years.

content.title: Using half-life
content.problem:
A radioactive substance has a half-life of 20 days. If you start with 
80 grams, how much remains after:

1. 20 days

2. 40 days

3. 60 days

4. 100 days
content.solution:
After each half-life, the amount is halved:

1. 20 days (1 half-life): $80 \div 2 = 40$ grams

2. 40 days (2 half-lives): $80 \div 4 = 20$ grams

3. 60 days (3 half-lives): $80 \div 8 = 10$ grams

4. 100 days (5 half-lives): $80 \div 32 = 2.5$ grams

General formula: $N = N_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t/t_{1/2}}$

content.title: World population growth
content.problem:
World population was approximately 6.1 billion in 2000 and 7.8 billion 
in 2020. Assuming exponential growth, find:

1. The growth constant $k$

2. The predicted population in 2050

3. The doubling time
content.solution:
Let $t = 0$ correspond to 2000, so $N_0 = 6.1$ (in billions).

**(a)** Using $N(20) = 7.8$:
\[
 7.8 = 6.1 e^{20k}
\]
\[
 \frac{7.8}{6.1} = e^{20k}
\]
\[
 \ln(1.279) = 20k
\]
\[
 k = \frac{0.246}{20} \approx 0.0123
\]

**(b)** Population in 2050 ($t = 50$):
\[
 N(50) = 6.1 e^{0.0123 \times 50} = 6.1 e^{0.615} \approx 6.1 \times 1.85 \approx 11.3 \text{ billion}
\]

**(c)** Doubling time:
\[
 t_{\text{double}} = \frac{\ln 2}{0.0123} \approx \frac{0.693}{0.0123} \approx 56 \text{ years}
\]

content.title: Stewardship of Creation
content.content:
Genesis 1:28 commands humanity to "be fruitful and multiply." Yet 
unlimited exponential growth in a finite world eventually faces limits. 
Wisdom requires balancing the mandate to flourish with responsible 
stewardship of resources. Understanding exponential growth mathematically 
helps us think wisely about sustainability, carrying capacity, and our 
responsibility to future generations.

content.title: Carbon-14 dating
content.problem:
An archaeologist finds a wooden artifact containing 35\% of its original 
carbon-14. How old is the artifact? (Half-life of C-14 is 5730 years.)
content.solution:
First, find $k$ from the half-life:
\[
 k = \frac{-\ln 2}{5730} \approx -0.000121
\]

If 35\% remains, then $N(t) = 0.35 N_0$:
\[
 0.35 N_0 = N_0 e^{-0.000121t}
\]
\[
 0.35 = e^{-0.000121t}
\]
\[
 \ln(0.35) = -0.000121t
\]
\[
 t = \frac{-1.050}{-0.000121} \approx 8680 \text{ years}
\]

The artifact is approximately 8,700 years old.

content.text:
When an object at one temperature is placed in an environment at a 
different temperature, it cools (or warms) exponentially.

content.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
content.content:
\[
 T(t) = T_s + (T_0 - T_s)e^{-kt}
\]
where:

- $T(t)$ = temperature at time $t$

- $T_s$ = surrounding temperature

- $T_0$ = initial temperature

- $k > 0$ = cooling constant

content.title: Cooling coffee
content.problem:
Coffee at 200°F is placed in a 70°F room. After 10 minutes, the coffee 
is 150°F. Find:

1. The cooling constant $k$

2. The temperature after 30 minutes

3. How long until the coffee reaches 100°F

content.title: Medication decay
content.problem:
A patient receives 400 mg of a medication. The drug has a half-life of 
4 hours in the body. How much remains after:

1. 8 hours

2. 12 hours

3. 24 hours

When does less than 25 mg remain?

content.title: Finding growth rate from data
content.problem:
An investment doubles in 9 years with continuous compounding. What is 
the annual interest rate?

content.title: Decay and Renewal
content.content:
Exponential decay models death, loss, and entropy—the tendency of 
ordered systems to decay. Carbon-14 decays; radioactive elements 
disintegrate; even memories fade over time.

Yet Scripture promises renewal: "Though outwardly we are wasting away, 
yet inwardly we are being renewed day by day" (2 Corinthians 4:16). 
The mathematics of decay is real, but so is the possibility of renewal. 
What decays in Adam can be renewed in Christ.

marginNotes.text:
A time to be born and a time to die, 
a time to plant and a time to uproot.
